title: Cloth Case Binding
subtitle: Full hard case binding in bookbinding cloth, with rounding and backing the spine
excerpt: A full cloth hardback: sew the text block, round and back the spine to create the characteristic convex profile and shoulder hinges, make a stiff cloth case, and case in. A multi-day project that produces a properly rounded, durable hardback book.
sourceNotes: Full case construction in cloth based on Cockerell, Douglas. Bookbinding and the Care of Books. London: Pitman, 1901.

projectSchedule.title: Day 0: Fold, collate, and sew
projectSchedule.body: Fold the text paper into signatures, collate in order, pierce the sewing stations, and sew the complete text block with a kettle-stitch structure. Leave under a light weight overnight.

projectSchedule.title: Day 1: Round and back the spine
projectSchedule.body: Glue up the spine with diluted PVA, allow to become touch-dry, then round with a hammer over a block. Back the spine to form the shoulders. Attach a mull lining and leave under a press until fully dry.

projectSchedule.title: Day 2: Make the case and case in
projectSchedule.body: Cut boards and spine strip, paste out and cover in bookbinding cloth, press until dry. Case in by pasting out the endpapers and joining the text block to the case. Press overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of shaping the spine of a glued-up text block into a gentle convex curve by rolling it over a backing hammer while applying light mallet blows. Rounding distributes the swell from sewing thread evenly and strengthens the book against bowing.

The process, following rounding, of forming shoulders along the edges of the spine by hammering the outermost signatures outward. The shoulders support the boards and form the basis for the hinge channel.
A loosely woven starched fabric glued to the spine of the text block to reinforce the joints between signatures and provide a surface for attaching the endpapers and boards.
A single folded unit of pages, typically 4 to 8 sheets, forming one section of the text block. Multiple signatures sewn together form the complete book block.

attrs.heading: Adhesive safety
attrs.body: PVA and cooked wheat-paste are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Rubber cement and spray adhesives need ventilation and are best used outside or by an open window.

attrs.heading: Sharp tools
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

content.text: A cloth case binding is the standard production hardback construction used since the nineteenth century. This version includes rounding and backing: the spine is first shaped into a convex curve (
rounding
), then the outermost
signatures
are hammered out to form shoulders (
backing
). The shoulders support the boards and create the hinge channel. Skipping these steps produces a functional but structurally weaker result.
Day 0: Sewing the text block
Step 1: Fold and collate

Fold sheets of 90 gsm cartridge into signatures of 4 sheets each (8 leaves, 16 pages). Use 8 to 12 signatures for a standard notebook or journal. Fold each group together, crease the fold firmly with the bone folder working from the centre outward. Collate in order.
Step 2: Pierce and sew

Mark five sewing stations on a card template: 10 mm from head, 10 mm from tail, and three evenly spaced between. Pierce all signatures using the template. Wax the linen thread. Sew through the stations with a standard kettle-stitch link: work each signature from inside outward, linking to the previous one with a kettle stitch at the head and tail stations. Tie off after the final signature. Leave under a light weight overnight.
Day 1: Rounding, backing, and lining
Step 3: Glue up the spine

Mix PVA with water to a consistency similar to single cream. Apply a thin coat to the spine with a stiff brush, working it between the signatures. Leave until touch-dry but not fully set: the spine should feel slightly rubbery, not wet or hard.
Step 4: Round the spine

Hold the text block with the spine facing up and one board face resting against your body. With the palm of the free hand and a steady rolling motion, push the top signatures forward and the bottom signatures back. The spine should develop a gentle arc. Work from the centre outward toward the head and tail.

If the block is stiff, use a hammer or mallet against a backing block: lay the block on a firm surface and use light tapping blows to encourage the curvature. The finished arc should be no more than one-third of the total spine thickness at its deepest point.
Step 5: Back the spine

Clamp the rounded text block in a lying press or between two boards in a book press so the spine projects 2 to 3 mm above the pressing boards. This exposed margin will become the shoulder. Using a backing hammer, strike the outermost signatures with angled blows, working from the centre of the spine toward the edges on each side. The signatures at each edge should fold over to form a right-angle shoulder against the top face of the pressing board.
The finished shoulders should be crisp and level. The board thickness should just fill the channel between the shoulder and the spine.
Step 6: Attach the mull
Cut a piece of
mull

(loosely woven bookbinding fabric) to the spine height minus 10 mm and the spine width plus 30 mm on each side. Apply a thin coat of PVA to the spine. Lay the mull centrally over the spine and press it down firmly. Fold the side wings of mull over the spine shoulders. Leave under a press until fully dry.
Day 2: Case construction and casing in
Step 7: Cut boards and spine strip

Measure the text block: height, page width, and spine width. Boards: 3 mm taller than the text block (1.5 mm overhang at head and tail), and 3 mm wider (overhang at foredge only). Spine strip: exact spine width and board height, cut from lighter card (1 mm). Leave a 5 mm gap between each board and the spine strip when positioning during covering.
Step 8: Cover the case in cloth

Cut the bookbinding cloth to size: total width of both boards plus spine plus the two hinge gaps, plus 30 mm turn-in on all sides. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA to the cloth (or use a nipping press to apply adhesive evenly). Lay the boards and spine strip in position on the cloth, maintaining the 5 mm hinge gap at each side of the spine strip.
Fold in the turn-in, mitreing the corners. Head and tail first, then foredge sides. Rub down all surfaces firmly through a waste sheet. Place under a press until fully dry.
Step 9: Attach endpapers

Fold two sheets of endpaper stock to page size. Paste a 5 mm strip along the spine edge of each and press one to each end of the text block, tight against the first and last signatures. The mull wings should sit between the endpaper and the outermost signature.
Step 10: Case in

Open the case flat. Paste out the top endpaper and the mull wing on the same side with PVA. Lay the text block into the case, spine aligned with the spine strip, and centre the overhangs at head, tail, and foredge. Press the pasted surfaces down firmly. Close the case, flip, open the back board, and paste out and press the second endpaper and mull wing.

Close the book. With a bone folder, press a groove along both hinges (the joints between boards and spine). Nip in the press for 10 minutes. Open carefully to check no pages have adhered; re-close and press overnight under boards and weights.

items.cause: PVA applied too thick or too wet, or text block left too long before rounding so adhesive set fully.
items.fix: Work at the touch-dry stage. If the spine has dried fully before rounding, dampen it lightly with a barely-damp sponge, wait 2 minutes, and try again.

items.symptom: Shoulders are uneven or one is higher than the other
items.cause: Text block not clamped symmetrically in the press, or unequal hammer blows on each side.
items.fix: Re-clamp symmetrically and check with a ruler before backing. Work both sides alternately in small increments.

items.cause: Mitre cut too far from the corner, leaving excess material that folds over itself.
items.fix: The mitre cut should be 1 to 2 mm from the corner of the board, not flush. Fold head/tail in first, pulling tightly; then fold the foredge sides.
